feed.title: Stories by Byung Min Choi (Paul) on Medium
feed.description: Stories by Byung Min Choi (Paul) on Medium

items.title: AWS-Native Observability on EKS Fargate: A Dual-Pipeline ADOT Architecture
items.author: Byung Min Choi (Paul)
items.description:
EKS Fargate removes the operational burden of managing worker nodes. No node patching. No EC2 lifecycle management. But it also removes the observability primitives most teams take for granted: you cannot run DaemonSets. That single constraint forces a complete rethink of how you collect infrastructure telemetry. This post documents the architecture I implemented for a production microservice platform running on EKS Fargate — and the specific decisions that made it work.

The Core Problem: No DaemonSet, No Node Agent

In a standard Kubernetes observability setup, you deploy a metrics agent as a DaemonSet
so that every node gets an instance. Node Exporter, Prometheus Agent, ADOT Collector
 — all assume node-level access.

On Fargate, that model breaks.

Fargate pods run on AWS-managed infrastructure. There are no joinable nodes.
DaemonSets are not scheduled.

This has two consequences:

  1. You cannot scrape cAdvisor via localhost or node IP
  2. You need a different deployment model for the ADOT Collector itself

For the collector deployment, the answer is a StatefulSet — a single ADOT Collector
instance running in its own namespace, scraping metrics via the Kubernetes API server.

For cAdvisor access, Fargate exposes container metrics through:

/api/v1/nodes/{node}/proxy/metrics/cadvisor

The collector authenticates using a ServiceAccount token and cluster CA certificate.
This is Fargate’s substitute for direct node agent access.

Architecture: Two Independent Pipelines

A single pipeline for all metrics creates two problems:

  • Infrastructure metrics and application metrics have different cardinality, labels, and lifecycle
  • Mixing them forces the same processors and exporters to handle incompatible schemas

The solution: two fully independent pipelines inside a single ADOT Collector instance.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EKS Fargate Cluster                                    │
│                                                         │
│  ┌────────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  ADOT Collector    │    │  App Pods (my-service)  │  │
│  │  (StatefulSet)     │◄───│  :8999/actuator/        │  │
│  │                    │    │  prometheus             │  │
│  │  Pipeline 1:       │◄───┤                         │  │
│  │  metrics/fargate   │    │  cAdvisor               │  │
│  │  (infrastructure)  │    │  (via k8s API)          │  │
│  │                    │    └─────────────────────────┘  │
│  │  Pipeline 2:       │                                 │
│  │  metrics/application│                                │
│  │  (JVM/HTTP/DB)     │                                 │
│  └────────┬───────────┘                                 │
│           │                                             │
│  ┌────────▼───────────┐                                 │
│  │  CloudWatch EMF    │                                 │
│  │  ContainerInsights │                                 │
│  └────────────────────┘                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Pipeline 1: Infrastructure Metrics (metrics/fargate)

This pipeline handles cAdvisor data and feeds CloudWatch Container Insights.

The processor chain is deliberately ordered:

receivers:
  prometheus/fargate:
    config:
      scrape_configs:
        - job_name: fargate-cadvisor
          scheme: https
          metrics_path: /api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/metrics/cadvisor
          tls_config:
            ca_file: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt
            insecure_skip_verify: true
          bearer_token_file: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
          scrape_interval: 1m
          scrape_timeout: 40s
processors:
  metricstransform/label_1:
    # Normalize labels to Container Insights schema
    # name -> container_id
    # kubernetes_io_hostname -> NodeName
    # eks_amazonaws_com_compute_type -> LaunchType
  metricstransform/rename:
    # Fork metrics into Container-level (new_container_*) and Pod-level (pod_*)
  filter/fargate:
    # Pass only new_container_* and pod_* patterns - drop everything else
  cumulativetodelta/fargate:
    # Convert CPU/network cumulative counters to delta
  deltatorate/fargate:
    # Convert delta to per-second rate
  metricsgeneration/1:
    # Derive: pod_network_total_bytes, pod_memory_utilization_over_pod_limit
    #         pod_cpu_usage_total, pod_cpu_limit
  metricsgeneration/2:
    # Derive: pod_cpu_utilization_over_pod_limit
  metricstransform/label_2:
    # Add Type: Pod / Type: Container labels
    # Rename new_container_* -> container_*
  batch/fargate:
    timeout: 60s
exporters:
  awsemf/fargate:
    log_group_name: /aws/containerinsights/${CLUSTER_NAME}/performance
    log_stream_name: "{PodName}"
    namespace: ContainerInsights
    eks_fargate_container_insights_enabled: true
    dimension_rollup_option: NoDimensionRollup
    metric_declarations:
      - dimensions: [[ClusterName, LaunchType]]
        metric_name_selectors: [pod_cpu_usage_total, pod_memory_working_set_bytes, ...]
      - dimensions: [[ClusterName, Namespace, PodName, LaunchType]]
        metric_name_selectors: [pod_cpu_usage_total, ...]

Why cumulativetodelta + deltatorate?

cAdvisor emits CPU time as a monotonically increasing counter. CloudWatch Container Insights
expects a per-second utilization rate. Without this transformation chain, your CPU graphs
show ever-increasing values instead of actual usage percentages.

Why metricsgeneration?

Pod-level derived metrics (pod_cpu_utilization_over_pod_limit) cannot be scraped directly.
They are calculated from raw cAdvisor values inside the processor chain. Skipping this step
means your Container Insights dashboards show empty panels for the most useful utilization metrics.

Pipeline 2: Application Metrics (metrics/application)

This pipeline scrapes Spring Boot Actuator endpoints and handles JVM, connection pool,
circuit breaker, and HTTP telemetry.

receivers:
  prometheus/application:
    config:
      scrape_configs:
        # Job 1: cAdvisor at container level (label-normalized)
        - job_name: kubelet-cadvisor-metrics
          # Excludes: container=POD and empty container labels
          # Relabels: container -> containerName, pod -> podName
        # Job 2: Spring Boot Actuator
        - job_name: spring-boot-actuator
          kubernetes_sd_configs:
            - role: pod
              namespaces:
                names: [my-service]
          relabel_configs:
            # Target port 8999, path /actuator/prometheus
            # Exclude: fluent-bit containers, ADOT namespace, ADOT itself
          metric_relabel_configs:
            # Rename metrics:
            # http_server_requests_seconds_count -> http_request_count_total
            # http_server_requests_seconds_sum   -> http_request_duration_seconds_total
            # Drop resilience4j series where state != "open"
processors:
  k8sattributes:
    # Inject: k8s.pod.name, k8s.namespace.name, k8s.pod.uid, k8s.node.name, app
  filter/essential_metrics:
    # Pass only the curated metric list (see below)
  batch/application:
    timeout: 60s
    send_batch_size: 1000
    send_batch_max_size: 1500
exporters:
  awsemf/application:
    log_group_name: /aws/containerinsights/${CLUSTER_NAME}/performance
    log_stream_name: "{podName}"
    namespace: ContainerInsights
    dimension_rollup_option: NoDimensionRollup
    metric_declarations:
      - dimensions: [[namespace, app, containerName, podName]]
        metric_name_selectors:
          - container_cpu_usage_seconds_total
          - jvm_memory_used_bytes
          - hikaricp_connections_active
          - tomcat_threads_busy_threads
          - ...
      - dimensions: [[namespace, app, containerName]]
        metric_name_selectors: [resilience4j_circuitbreaker_state]
      - dimensions: [[namespace, app, containerName, uri, status, method]]
        metric_name_selectors: [http_request_count_total, http_request_duration_seconds_total]

What metrics are actually collected?

The filter processor passes only these categories:

Raw Spring Actuator output contains hundreds of time series. This curated list drops
roughly 70% of what Prometheus exposes — keeping only what drives operational decisions.

IRSA: The Permission Model

ADOT needs CloudWatch write access. On Fargate, the right approach is
IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), not instance profiles.

# otel-sa.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: adot-collector
  namespace: fargate-container-insights
  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::<ACCOUNT_ID>:role/EKS-Fargate-ADOT-ServiceAccount-Role

The IAM role needs:

{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": [
    "logs:CreateLogGroup",
    "logs:CreateLogStream",
    "logs:PutLogEvents",
    "logs:DescribeLogStreams"
  ],
  "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:*:*:*"
}

The ClusterRole grants read access to nodes, pods, endpoints, configmaps,
and crucially — nonResourceURLs: ["/metrics/cadvisor"].
Without the nonResource URL permission, cAdvisor scraping silently fails.

Resource Sizing

StatefulSet resource allocation:

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "1"
    memory: 2Gi
  requests:
    cpu: "1"
    memory: 2Gi

Requests equal limits. On Fargate, this matters:
Fargate allocates compute based on the highest of pod-level requests and container limits.
Undersizing causes OOMKilled during high-cardinality scrape cycles.

Logging: Two Separate Paths

Fargate has a built-in Fluent Bit sidecar that reads stdout/stderr automatically.
This handles operational logs to CloudWatch:

# aws-logging ConfigMap (namespace: aws-observability)
[FILTER]
    Name    rewrite_tag
    Match   kube.*
    Rule    $kubernetes['container_name'] external-provider external-provider false
[OUTPUT]
    Name              cloudwatch_logs
    Match             external-provider
    log_group_name    external-provider
    log_stream_prefix from-fluent-bit-
[OUTPUT]
    Name              cloudwatch_logs
    Match             kube.*
    log_group_name    my-service
    log_stream_prefix from-fluent-bit-

Application file logs (/app/logs/application.log) need a separate sidecar Fluent Bit
to reach Kafka for downstream log aggregation:

[INPUT]
    Name        tail
    Path        /app/logs/application.log
    Buffer_Chunk_Size 1MB
    Buffer_Max_Size   2MB
[FILTER]
    Name    record_modifier
    Record  logname app-service
    Record  host    ${HOSTNAME}
[OUTPUT]
    Name    kafka
    brokers <kafka-brokers>:9094
    topics  app-logs
    rdkafka.security.protocol   SASL_PLAINTEXT
    rdkafka.sasl.mechanisms     SCRAM-SHA-256
    rdkafka.compression.codec   lz4

What This Actually Solves

The dual-pipeline architecture gives you independent control over:

  • Scrape intervals: infrastructure at 1m, application can be different
  • Processor chains: cumulative-to-delta only needed for infrastructure
  • Dimension sets: HTTP metrics need uri + status + method, JVM metrics do not
  • Cost control: filter/essential_metrics before export, not after

The Fargate constraint that initially looks like a limitation ends up producing a
cleaner observability design than most DaemonSet-based setups.
Forced intentionality is not a bad thing.

Summary

This stack runs in production across multiple regions.
The patterns here are directly transferable to any Spring Boot workload on EKS Fargate.

EKS Fargate removes the operational burden of managing worker nodes. No node patching. No EC2 lifecycle management. But it also removes the observability primitives most teams take for granted: you cannot run DaemonSets. That single constraint forces a complete rethink of how you collect infrastructure telemetry. This post documents the architecture I implemented for a production microservice platform running on EKS Fargate — and the specific decisions that made it work.

The Core Problem: No DaemonSet, No Node Agent

In a standard Kubernetes observability setup, you deploy a metrics agent as a DaemonSet

Fargate pods run on AWS-managed infrastructure. There are no joinable nodes.

This has two consequences:

  1. You cannot scrape cAdvisor via localhost or node IP

For the collector deployment, the answer is a StatefulSet — a single ADOT Collector

For cAdvisor access, Fargate exposes container metrics through:

The collector authenticates using a ServiceAccount token and cluster CA certificate.

  • Infrastructure metrics and application metrics have different cardinality, labels, and lifecycle

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EKS Fargate Cluster                                    │
│                                                         │
│  ┌────────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  ADOT Collector    │    │  App Pods (my-service)  │  │
│  │  (StatefulSet)     │◄───│  :8999/actuator/        │  │
│  │                    │    │  prometheus             │  │
│  │  Pipeline 1:       │◄───┤                         │  │
│  │  metrics/fargate   │    │  cAdvisor               │  │
│  │  (infrastructure)  │    │  (via k8s API)          │  │
│  │                    │    └─────────────────────────┘  │
│  │  Pipeline 2:       │                                 │
│  │  metrics/application│                                │
│  │  (JVM/HTTP/DB)     │                                 │
│  └────────┬───────────┘                                 │
│           │                                             │
│  ┌────────▼───────────┐                                 │
│  │  CloudWatch EMF    │                                 │
│  │  ContainerInsights │                                 │
│  └────────────────────┘                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Pipeline 1: Infrastructure Metrics (metrics/fargate)

This pipeline handles cAdvisor data and feeds CloudWatch Container Insights.

The processor chain is deliberately ordered:

receivers:
  prometheus/fargate:
    config:
      scrape_configs:
        - job_name: fargate-cadvisor
          scheme: https
          metrics_path: /api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/metrics/cadvisor
          tls_config:
            ca_file: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt
            insecure_skip_verify: true
          bearer_token_file: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
          scrape_interval: 1m
          scrape_timeout: 40s
processors:
  metricstransform/label_1:
    # Normalize labels to Container Insights schema
    # name -> container_id
    # kubernetes_io_hostname -> NodeName
    # eks_amazonaws_com_compute_type -> LaunchType

  cumulativetodelta/fargate:

  metricsgeneration/1:
    # Derive: pod_network_total_bytes, pod_memory_utilization_over_pod_limit

  metricsgeneration/2:
    # Derive: pod_cpu_utilization_over_pod_limit

      - dimensions: [[ClusterName, LaunchType]]

Pipeline 2: Application Metrics (metrics/application)

This pipeline scrapes Spring Boot Actuator endpoints and handles JVM, connection pool,

receivers:
  prometheus/application:
    config:
      scrape_configs:
        # Job 1: cAdvisor at container level (label-normalized)
        - job_name: kubelet-cadvisor-metrics
          # Excludes: container=POD and empty container labels
          # Relabels: container -> containerName, pod -> podName

            - role: pod

            # Exclude: fluent-bit containers, ADOT namespace, ADOT itself
          metric_relabel_configs:

            # http_server_requests_seconds_count -> http_request_count_total
            # http_server_requests_seconds_sum   -> http_request_duration_seconds_total
            # Drop resilience4j series where state != "open"
processors:
  k8sattributes:
    # Inject: k8s.pod.name, k8s.namespace.name, k8s.pod.uid, k8s.node.name, app
  filter/essential_metrics:
    # Pass only the curated metric list (see below)
  batch/application:
    timeout: 60s
    send_batch_size: 1000
    send_batch_max_size: 1500
exporters:
  awsemf/application:
    log_group_name: /aws/containerinsights/${CLUSTER_NAME}/performance
    log_stream_name: "{podName}"
    namespace: ContainerInsights
    dimension_rollup_option: NoDimensionRollup
    metric_declarations:
      - dimensions: [[namespace, app, containerName, podName]]
        metric_name_selectors:
          - container_cpu_usage_seconds_total
          - jvm_memory_used_bytes
          - hikaricp_connections_active
          - tomcat_threads_busy_threads
          - ...
      - dimensions: [[namespace, app, containerName]]
        metric_name_selectors: [resilience4j_circuitbreaker_state]
      - dimensions: [[namespace, app, containerName, uri, status, method]]
        metric_name_selectors: [http_request_count_total, http_request_duration_seconds_total]

What metrics are actually collected?

The filter processor passes only these categories:

Raw Spring Actuator output contains hundreds of time series. This curated list drops
roughly 70% of what Prometheus exposes — keeping only what drives operational decisions.

IRSA: The Permission Model

ADOT needs CloudWatch write access. On Fargate, the right approach is
IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), not instance profiles.

# otel-sa.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: adot-collector
  namespace: fargate-container-insights
  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::<ACCOUNT_ID>:role/EKS-Fargate-ADOT-ServiceAccount-Role

The IAM role needs:

{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": [
    "logs:CreateLogGroup",
    "logs:CreateLogStream",
    "logs:PutLogEvents",
    "logs:DescribeLogStreams"
  ],
  "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:*:*:*"
}

The ClusterRole grants read access to nodes, pods, endpoints, configmaps,
and crucially — nonResourceURLs: ["/metrics/cadvisor"].
Without the nonResource URL permission, cAdvisor scraping silently fails.

Resource Sizing

  limits:

    memory: 2Gi

Fargate allocates compute based on the highest of pod-level requests and container limits.

Fargate has a built-in Fluent Bit sidecar that reads stdout/stderr automatically.
This handles operational logs to CloudWatch:

# aws-logging ConfigMap (namespace: aws-observability)
[FILTER]
    Name    rewrite_tag
    Match   kube.*
    Rule    $kubernetes['container_name'] external-provider external-provider false
[OUTPUT]
    Name              cloudwatch_logs
    Match             external-provider
    log_group_name    external-provider
    log_stream_prefix from-fluent-bit-
[OUTPUT]
    Name              cloudwatch_logs
    Match             kube.*

    log_stream_prefix from-fluent-bit-

[INPUT]
    Name        tail
    Path        /app/logs/application.log
    Buffer_Chunk_Size 1MB
    Buffer_Max_Size   2MB
[FILTER]
    Name    record_modifier
    Record  logname app-service
    Record  host    ${HOSTNAME}
[OUTPUT]
    Name    kafka
    brokers <kafka-brokers>:9094
    topics  app-logs
    rdkafka.security.protocol   SASL_PLAINTEXT
    rdkafka.sasl.mechanisms     SCRAM-SHA-256

The dual-pipeline architecture gives you independent control over:

items.title: Building a Thin DevOps Agent on AWS with Self-Hosted n8n, Lambda, and Slack
items.author: Byung Min Choi (Paul)
items.description:
Operational automation often grows by adding more tools: another dashboard, another SaaS product, another internal portal, another place engineers need to check during an incident.

That is still automation, but it does not always simplify operations.

This project started from a different question:

What is the smallest AWS-backed operating loop that can run real operational actions without adding another platform?

The answer was intentionally simple:

Slack is the operator interface.
n8n routes the workflow.
Lambda executes the operational action.
CloudWatch Logs keeps the execution record.

The first implemented action is a health check for a self-hosted n8n instance running on Amazon EC2. The check does not simply ask whether EC2 is alive. It verifies whether the application endpoint is actually responding.

That distinction matters.

An EC2 instance can be healthy while the application is hung. Infrastructure health is not always application health.

The AWS side of the stack is small:

  • Amazon EC2 runs n8n with Docker Compose
  • Amazon EBS stores workflow data
  • Elastic IP provides a stable endpoint
  • AWS SAM deploys the Lambda action
  • EventBridge can trigger scheduled checks
  • CloudWatch Logs captures structured output

The Lambda returns a compact result:

{
  "ok": true,
  "status": 200,
  "latency_ms": 123
}

This result can be used in two ways.

Scheduled mode:

EventBridge → Lambda → CloudWatch Logs

ChatOps mode:

Slack → n8n → Lambda → Slack

The same action layer supports both automated checks and operator-triggered commands.

One interesting implementation detail is that the Lambda uses Strands Agents SDK without invoking an LLM.

There is no prompt.
No model call.
No Amazon Bedrock dependency.
No token cost.

The SDK is used only as a structured tool runtime:

agent = Agent(tools=[http_request])
response = agent.tool.http_request(
    method="GET",
    url=HEALTHCHECK_URL,
    timeout=10,
)

That is important for operations.

When an operator asks for a health check, I do not want creativity. I want deterministic behavior: the same timeout, the same action, and the same response shape every time.

This is why I think of the pattern as a thin DevOps Agent.

A script runs an action.
A DevOps Agent changes where operators go to act.

The operator does not need to know which Lambda runs, where the n8n server lives, or which endpoint represents health. They stay in Slack, and the workflow handles the rest.

The pattern can grow from here: log inspection, service restart, EC2 diagnostics, ticket creation, or deployment triggers.

But the principle should stay the same:

A DevOps Agent is not valuable because it does more things. It is valuable when it makes operations simpler to run.

Building a Thin DevOps Agent on AWS with Self-Hosted n8n, Lambda, and Slack was originally published in The Generator on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

EventBridge → Lambda → CloudWatch Logs

ChatOps mode:

Slack → n8n → Lambda → Slack

The same action layer supports both automated checks and operator-triggered commands.

One interesting implementation detail is that the Lambda uses Strands Agents SDK without invoking an LLM.

There is no prompt.
No model call.
No Amazon Bedrock dependency.
No token cost.

The SDK is used only as a structured tool runtime:

agent = Agent(tools=[http_request])
response = agent.tool.http_request(
    method="GET",
    url=HEALTHCHECK_URL,
    timeout=10,
)

That is important for operations.

When an operator asks for a health check, I do not want creativity. I want deterministic behavior: the same timeout, the same action, and the same response shape every time.

This is why I think of the pattern as a thin DevOps Agent.

A script runs an action.
A DevOps Agent changes where operators go to act.

The operator does not need to know which Lambda runs, where the n8n server lives, or which endpoint represents health. They stay in Slack, and the workflow handles the rest.

The pattern can grow from here: log inspection, service restart, EC2 diagnostics, ticket creation, or deployment triggers.

But the principle should stay the same:

A DevOps Agent is not valuable because it does more things. It is valuable when it makes operations simpler to run.

Building a Thin DevOps Agent on AWS with Self-Hosted n8n, Lambda, and Slack was originally published in The Generator on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Democratizing Branding: How Brandy Makes Professional Branding Accessible
items.author: Byung Min Choi (Paul)
items.description:
From weeks and thousands of dollars to minutes and cents — AI-powered branding for everyone

Introduction

Small business owners often face a harsh reality: professional branding — signboards, interiors, name generation, identity design — typically costs thousands of dollars and takes weeks of back-and-forth. The project Brandy addresses exactly this gap: what if branding could be done in five minutes, for under a dollar, without sacrificing professionalism? (See its Devpost listing.)

In this post, I’ll outline the impact of Brandy (why it matters) and then unpack the technical architecture & innovations behind it.

Part I — Impact: Why Brandy Matters

Leveling the Playing Field

Brandy empowers small and micro-businesses — those who historically could not afford high-end branding agencies — to access high-quality branding packages.

By reducing cost from thousands of dollars to approximately $0.20 per workflow, Brandy shifts the economics of branding.

This means local cafés, pop-ups, start-ups across the world can now compete visually with larger enterprises.

Time-to-Brand Dramatically Shortened

Traditional branding cycles span weeks; Brandy’s workflow completes in about 5 minutes.

In practice: name generation (~30 s), image generation (~60 s), full report delivered (~300 s) per the project metrics.

This rapid turnaround supports agile start-ups, pop-ups, experimental store formats, and iterating brand identity in real time.

Global and Scalable

Because it’s built serverless and uses AI agents, Brandy scales globally without needing expensive human consultants.

It’s designed for “brand everywhere” — signboards, interiors, marketing assets — enabling expansion across locales and languages (future roadmap) with minimal incremental cost.

Quality and Creativity Maintained

Despite the low cost and speed, Brandy doesn’t compromise on creativity — the system generates unique design styles, interior recommendations, cost estimates, and polished reports.

In other words: democratization without diluting value.

Business Model & Market Potential

For a small monthly fee or pay-per-workflow model, Brandy could become a SaaS platform for branding.

The addressable market: millions of SMBs globally, each needing brand identity, store signage, interiors.

By embedding branding into the start-up workflow, Brandy could shift the “design agency” model to “branding as a service”.

Part II — Technical Deep Dive: How Brandy Works

Architecture Overview

Brandy is implemented as a 7-agent server-less system on the Amazon Bedrock and AWS ecosystem.

Key components:

  • UI using Streamlit (running on ECS Fargate) for user interaction.
  • API Gateway (HTTP API) → multiple Lambda functions (agents) → DynamoDB + S3 for state and assets.
  • Core LLMs: Claude 4 Sonnet for text reasoning, Titan Image Generator v2 for image creation.

Agent Breakdown

Here are the responsibilities of each agent:

  • Supervisor Agent: orchestrates workflow, error recovery, central logging.
  • Product Insight Agent: analyses business/product/industry context.
  • Market Analyst Agent: regional market trends, insights.
  • Reporter Agent: generates business-name candidates, scores them (pronunciation, memorability, relevance, search engine).
  • Signboard Agent: creates signboard design styles/images.
  • Interior Agent: proposes interior design concepts (color schemes, materials, furniture, cost estimate).
  • Report Generator Agent: compiles everything into a downloadable HTML report.

Key Technical Innovations

Dynamic Style Generation

Rather than a fixed set of design templates, Brandy uses the LLM to generate contextual styles based on business name, industry, region. Example: for “Pokemon Concept Ramen” → styles like “anime”, “vibrant”, “playful”.

This yields richer, bespoke design suggestions.

Intelligent Score Validation

When generating business-name candidates, the system computes scores (pronunciation, memorability, relevance, search) and implements fallback algorithms for edge-cases (e.g., when LLM returns 0.0).

Example formula for pronunciation score:

S_pronunciation = 100 − length_penalty − complexity_penalty  
length_penalty = max(0, |length−10| ×2)  
complexity_penalty = uppercase_count×5 + digit_count×5

Prompt Optimization Pipeline

Image-generation prompts must respect model limits (Titan image generator has a 512-char max). The system builds concise prompts, removes optional details if limit exceeded, then truncates if still too long.

This ensures consistent success in image generation even for complex themes.

Performance & Cost Metrics

  • Name generation: ~30 s (3 names in one API call)
  • Image generation: ~60 s (3 images)
  • Total workflow: ~300 s (~5 min)
  • Cost per workflow: ~$0.20
  • Bedrock Claude (5 calls): $0.075
  • Titan Image (3 images): $0.12
  • Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, API Gateway: ~$0.005
  • Reliability: image generation success ~98%, score validation ~97%

Infrastructure as Code & Deployment

  • Used AWS SAM for IaC: full backend deploy with sam deploy — guided.
  • Frontend deploy script via ECS Fargate.
  • DynamoDB session management with TTL (24h) for state cleanup.
  • Structured logging and monitoring with CloudWatch.

Challenges & How They Were Addressed

  • Prompt length limit: Titan image generator enforces 512 characters; truncated prompts improved success rate to ~100%.
  • Invalid scores from the LLM: handled via fallback algorithm for scoring.
  • Async processing timeouts: name generation could exceed 30 s; resolved with async mode and polling.
  • Image selection mismatch in report: fixed data-flow so user’s selected image is properly preserved.

Part III — What We Learned & Next Steps

Lessons Learned

  • Prompt engineering: concise prompts beat verbose ones.
  • Agent-based architecture helps separation of concerns and maintainability.
  • Serverless cost optimization: batching calls, caching, limiting function sizes.
  • UX matters: showing progress, handling errors gracefully, asynchronous flows improve user experience.

Roadmap & Future Enhancements

  • Multi-language support: extend analysis and design to regions beyond English.
  • Advanced customization: user-upload logos, custom color palettes, brand voice adjustments.
  • Social media integration: generate social copy, marketing assets, graphics for campaign.
  • Brand evolution tracking: build an agent memory to support long-term brand growth.

Conclusion

Brandy demonstrates how modern AI tools and server-less architectures can democratize high-quality branding. With innovative prompt engineering, agent-based design, and efficient infrastructure, we’ve gone from a costly, lengthy process to one that’s fast, affordable, and scalable.

For small businesses, this means they no longer have to compromise based on budget — they can access professional identity design, signboards, interiors, branding reports — all in minutes.

For technologists and architects, Brandy is an example of how to build purpose-driven, scalable AI services using cloud-native components and LLMs.

As we roll into a world where branding is no longer a luxury, but a service, Brandy stands as a proof-point: AI-powered services aren’t just theoretical — they are real, accessible, and already solving real problems.

Democratizing Branding: How Brandy Makes Professional Branding Accessible was originally published in The Generator on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.
